title: 第 27 節
en: 11. The Pravaradhy ayah ; Pravaradhayah (Vyasa, No. 7.)
12. The Uktha-sastram ; Sastram (Vyasa, No. 8).
13. The Kratusankhya; Kratu (Vyasa, No. 9).
14. The Nigamah ; Agamah (Vyasa, No. 10).
15. The Yajnaparsve or parsvam ; Yajnam (Vyasa, No. 11) ; Parsvan (Vyasa, No. 12).
16. The Hautrakam; Hautrakam (Vyasa, No. 13).
17. The Prasavotthanam ; Pasavah (Vyasa, No. 14); Ukthani, (Vyasa, No. 15).
18. The Kurmalakshanam ; Kurmalakshanam, (Vyasa, No. 16).

A similar order has evidently been followed in a collection of the Parisishtas, forming part of Professor Wilson's valuable collection of MSS., now deposited in the Bodleian Library. The MS., however, is incomplete, and seems to have been copied by a person ignorant of Sanskrit from another MS., the leaves of which had been in confusion. Most of the MSS. of these Parisishtas are carelessly copied, whereas the MSS. of the Sutras are generally in excellent condition. The MSS. which Raja Radhakantadeva used seem to have been in an equally bad state, if we may judge from the various readings which he occasionally mentions.1 But although the Bodleian MS. leaves much to desire, it serves at least to support the authenticity of the titles given in the MS. of the Charanavyuha against the blunders of the printed text. We find there :
1 For instance M K ^ I H^V^fTjf^ V\~<51 I instead of TJT- PARISISHTAS. 255 1. The Yupalakshanam,1 a short treatise on the manner of preparing the sacrificial post.
2. The Chhagalakshanam,2 on animals fit for sacrifice.

3. The Pratijna,3 begins with giving some definition of sacrificial terms, but breaks off with the fourth leaf, whereas the Pravaradhyaya (No. 11) had already been commenced on the third, and is afterwards carried on on the fifth leaf. Thus we lose from the fourth to the eleventh Parisishta, which formed part of the original MS. if we may judge from the fact that the Pravaradhyaya is here also called the eleventh Parisishta.
4. The Anuvakasankhya exists in MS. E.I.H. 965.
5. The Charanavyuhah is found in numerous copies.

6. The Sraddhakalpah exists in MS. E. I. H. 1201, and MS. Chambers 66. It is there ascribed to Katyayana. There is also among the Chambers MSS. at Berlin (292 — 294) a Sraddha-kalpa-bhashya ascribed to Gobhila.
7. The Sulvikani are found in MS. Chambers 66, and a gulvadipika, MS. E. I. H. 1678.

8. The Parshadam. This must not be mistaken for a Pratisakhya, nor would it be right to call the Pratisakhyas Parisishtas. The Parshada is a much smaller work, as may be seen from a MS. in the Royal Library at Berlin, Chambers 378.
9. The Rigyajiinshi is the only Parisishta that cannot be verified in MS. ; there is no reason for supposing that it was an Anukramani either of the Yajur-veda or Rig-veda.
10. The Ishtakapuranam has been preserved in 1 MS. Chambers, 66.
2 MS. Chambers, 66.
3 Called Pratishthalakshanam in MS. Chambers, 66.
MS. Chambers 389 with a commentary by Karka, and in MS. Chambers 392, with a commentary by Yaj nikadeva.

11. The Pravaradhyayah is found again in our own MS., and is followed by a small tract, the Gotranirnayah. The seven principal Pravaras are those of the Bhrigus, Angiras, Visvamitras, Vasishthas, Kasyapas, Atris, and Agastis. The eight founders of Gotras or families are Jamadagni, Bharadvaja, Visvamitra, Atri, Gautama, Vasishtha, Kasyapa and Agastya.1 The whole treatise, of which more hereafter, is ascribed to Katyayana.2 12. The Uktha-£astram is found in our MS. So is 13. The Kratusankhya, which gives an enumeration of the principal sacrifices.

14. The Nigama-parisishta is the last in our MS. It contains a number of Yedic words with their explanations, and forms a useful appendix to Yaska's Nirukta. It alludes not only to the four castes, but the names of the mixed castes also, according to the Anuloma and Pratiloma order, are mentioned.
The four last Parisishtas are wanting in our MS.

The fifteenth, however, the Yajnaparsvam is found in MS. E. I. H. 1729, Chambers, 358 ; the sixteenth, the Hautrakam, exists with a commentary in MS. Chambers 669. The two last Parisishtas have not yet been met with in MS., but we may probably DECLINE OF BRAHMANISM. 257 form some idea of the last, the Kurmalakshanam, from some chapters of Varahamihira's Brihatsanhita, where we find both a Kurrnavibhagah. and a Kurmalakshanam, the last being there followed by a chapter, called by the same name as the second Parisishta, Chhagalakshanam.

Although there is little of real importance to be learned from these Parisishtas, the fact of their existence is important in the history of the progress and decay of the Hindu mind. As in the first or Chhandas period, we see the Aryan settlers of India giving free utterance to their thoughts and feelings, and thus creating unconsciously a whole world of religious, moral, and political ideas ; as we find them again during the second or Mantra period, carefully collecting their harvest ; and during the third or Brahmana period busily occupied in systematising and interpreting the strains of their forefathers, which had already become unintelligible and sacred ; as in the fourth or Sutra period we see their whole energy employed in simplifying the complicated system of the theology and the ceremonial of the Brahmanas ; so we shall have to recognise in these Parisishtas a new phase of the Indian mind, marked by a distinct character, which must admit of historical explanation. The object of the Parisishtas is to supply information on theological or ceremonial points which had been passed over in the Sutras, most likely because they were not deemed of sufficient importance, or because they were supposed to be well known to those more immediately concerned. But what most distinguishes the Parisishtas from the Sutras is this, that they treat everything in a popular and superficial manner : as if the time was gone, when students would spend ten or twenty years of their lives 258 DECLINE OF BRAHMANTSM.

in fathoming the mysteries and mastering the intricacies of the Brahmana literature. A party driven to such publications as the Parisishtas, is a party fighting a losing battle. We see no longer that self-complacent spirit which pervades the Brahmanas. The authors of the Brahmanas felt that whatever they said must be believed, whatever they ordained must be obeyed. They are frightened by no absurdity, and the word " impossible " seems to have been banished from their dictionary. In the Sutras we see that a change has taken place. Their authors seem to feel that the public which they address will no longer listen to endless theological swaggering. There may have been deep wisdom in the Brahmanas, and their authors may have sincerely believed in all they said ; but they evidently calculated on a submissiveness on the part of their pupils or readers, which only exists in countries domineered over by priests or pro* fessors. The authors of the Sutras have learned that people will not listen to wisdom unless it is clothed in a garb of clear argument and communicated in intelligible language. Their works contain all that is essential in the Brahmanas, but they give it in a practical, concise and definite form. These works were written at a time when the Brahman s were fighting their first battles against the popular doctrines of Buddha. They were not yet afraid. Their language is firm, though it is no longer inflated. " Buddhism," as Burnouf says,1 " soon grew into a system of easy devotion, and found numerous recruits among those who were frightened by the difficulties of Brahmanical science. At the same time that 1 Burnouf, Introduction a l'Histoire du Buddhisme. Roth, Abhandlungen, p. 22.

DECLINE OF BRAHMANISM. 259 Buddhism attracted the ignorant among theBrahmans, it received with open arms the poor and the miserable of all classes." It was to remove, or at least to simplify, the difficulties of their teaching, that men like Saunaka and Katyayana adopted the novel style of the Sutras. Such changes in the sacred literature of a people are not made without an object, and the object of the Sutras, as distinct from that of the Brahmanas, could be no other than to offer practical manuals to those who were discouraged by too elaborate treatises, and who had found a shorter way to salvation opened to them by the heretical preaching of Buddha. After the Sutras there is no literature of a purely Yedic character except the Parisishtas. They still presuppose the laws of the Sutras and the faith of the Brahmanas. There is as yet no trace of any definite supremacy being accorded to Siva or Vishnu or Brahman. New gods, however, are mentioned ; vulgar or popular ceremonies are alluded to. The castes have become more marked and multiplied. The whole intellectual atmosphere is still Yedic, and the Vedic ceremonial, the Yedic theology, the Yedic language seem still to absorb the thoughts of the authors of the Parisishtas. Any small matter that had been overlooked by the authors of the Sutras is noted down as a matter of grave importance. Subjects on which general instructions were formerly considered sufficient, are now treated in special treatises, intended for men who would no longer take the trouble of reading the whole system of the Brahmanic ceremonial. The technical and severe language of the Sutras was exchanged for a free and easy style, whether in prose or metre ; and however near in time the Brahmans may place the authors of s 2 260 RISE OF BUDDHISM.

the Sutras and some of the Parisishtas, certain it is that no man who had mastered the Sutra style would ever have condescended to employ the slovenly diction of the Parisishtas. The change in the position and the characters of the Brahman s, such as we find them in the Sutras, and such as we find them again in the Parisishtas, has been rapid and decisive. The men who could write such works were aware of their own weakness, and had probably suffered many defeats. The world around them was moving in a new direction, and the old Vedic age died away in impotent twaddle.
zh: 11. 《普拉瓦拉經》(Pravaradhy ayah)；普拉瓦拉經(Pravaradhayah)（《毗耶娑書》第七號）
12. 《烏克塔經》(Uktha-sastram)；經論 (Vyasa, No. 8)。
13. 力量數目說；力量（毘耶娑，第9條）。
14. 《尼迦摩》；《阿迦摩》（毘耶娑，第10號）。
15. 《雅惹那帕爾濕韋》或帕爾濕瓦；《雅惹那》（《韋亞娑》，第11號）；《帕爾濕瓦》（《韋亞娑》，第12號）。
16. 豪特剌咖姆；豪特剌咖姆（毗耶娑，第 13 號）。
17. 普拉薩瓦烏塔那姆（The Prasavotthanam）；帕薩瓦（Pasavah）（《毗耶娑經》第14）；烏克塔尼（Ukthani）（《毗耶娑經》第15）。
18. 《龜相經》；《龜相經》，（毘耶娑，第16號）。

一部類似的編排順序在帕里西什塔斯文獻的一份手抄本中顯然得到遵循，該手抄本是威爾遜教授寶貴的手抄本蒐集的一部分，目前存放於博德利圖書館。然而，該手抄本並不完整，似乎是由一位不懂梵文的人從另一份手抄本抄錄而來，而那份手抄本的葉片曾經散亂混淆。大多數這類帕里西什塔斯的手抄本都抄錄得很不謹慎，而蘇特拉的手抄本通常狀況良好。羅闍羅陀坎塔德瓦所使用的手抄本似乎也處於同樣惡劣的狀態，從他偶爾提及的各種異文我們可以判斷得出。¹ 但儘管博德利手抄本存在許多缺憾，它至少可以用來支持《車若那毘闍》手抄本中所給予的書名的真正性，以對抗印刷本中的錯誤。我們在那裡發現：
例如《牙婆羅克沙那經》(Yupalakshanam)，是一部關於祭祀柱準備方式的簡短論述。
2. 《畜牲特徵論》（Chhagalakshanam）——論述堪作祭祀之畜牲。
3. 《婆闍提尼亞》(Pratijna)3 開始於對祭祀用語的若干定義，但在第四葉處中斷，而《普婆羅陀亞耶》(Pravaradhyaya)（第11部分）已在第三葉開始，隨後在第五葉繼續進行。因此我們從第四至第十一《附篇》(Parisishta)之間的部分喪失，這些本應是原稿的組成部分——若以《普婆羅陀亞耶》在此處亦被稱為第十一《附篇》這一事實判斷，我們即可認定此點。
4. 《經頌數論》(Anuvakasankhya)存在於東印度公司手稿 E.I.H. 965 中。
5. 《韻律經》（Charanavyuhah）存在於眾多的抄本中。
6. 《信願法則》(Sraddhakalpah) 存在於E. I. H. 1201 手稿及Chambers 66手稿中。在該手稿中被歸屬於迦堤耶延(Katyayana)。在柏林Chambers手稿中(292-294)亦有一部《信願法則釋》(Sraddha-kalpa-bhashya)被歸屬於高比羅(Gobhila)。
7. 《繩法經》（Sulvikani）見於 MS. Chambers 66，以及《繩法明燈》（gulvadipika）見於 MS. E. I. H. 1678。
8. 《帕爾沙達姆》。此書不可與《普拉蒂沙卡亞》混淆，反之亦然，將《普拉蒂沙卡亞》稱為《帕里希什塔》也不妥當。《帕爾沙達》是一部規模遠小得多的著作，柏林皇家圖書館所藏的手稿（Chambers 378號）足以為證。
9. 《梨俱夜柔尼施》（Rigyajninshi）是唯一無法在手稿中得到驗證的附篇；沒有理由假定它是《夜柔吠陀》（Yajur-veda）或《梨俱吠陀》的索引書（Anukramani）。
10. 《伊什塔卡往世書》（Ishtakapuranam）保存於1份手稿中。Chambers, 66.
2 MS. Chambers, 66.
3 在《錢伯斯手稿》中稱為 Pratishthalakshanam，第 66 頁。
MS. Chambers 389 附有迦爾卡（Karka）的注疏，MS. Chambers 392 則附有耶祇尼迦提婆（Yajnikadeva）的注疏。

11. 《Pravaradhyayah》在我們的抄本中亦有所載，其後緊跟著一部小論著《Gotranirnayah》。七個主要的 Pravara 分別屬於 Bhrigus、Angiras、Visvamitras、Vasishthas、Kasyapas、Atris 和 Agastis。八位氏族（Gotra）或家族的創立者為 Jamadagni、Bharadvaja、Visvamitra、Atri、Gautama、Vasishtha、Kasyapa 和 Agastya。¹整部論著，其詳情另述於下，乃歸於 Katyayana 所作。² 12. 《Uktha-£astram》亦在我們的抄本中所見。13. 《Kratusankhya》亦然，該書列舉了主要的祭祀儀式。
14. 《尼迦摩附篇》（Nigama-parisishta）是我們手稿中最後的一部。它含有眾多吠陀詞彙及其解釋，構成了雅斯卡《尼魯克塔》的有用附錄。它不僅涉及四種姓，並且根據順婚（Anuloma）與逆婚（Pratiloma）的順序，也提及了混雜種姓的名稱。
我們的古抄本中缺少最後四部《帕里西什塔經》（Parisishtas）。

第十五部《祭祀旁述》（Yajnaparsvam）見於MS. E. I. H. 1729，Chambers 358；第十六部《豪特剌迦》（Hautrakam）在MS. Chambers 669中存有注疏本。最後兩部《附篇》（Parisishtas）尚未在手稿中發現，但我們或可從瓦剌訶米希拉（Varahamihira）的《大典集》（Brihatsanhita）中的某些章節對最後一部《龜之特徵》（Kurmalakshanam）略窺一二，其中我們既發現有《龜之分章》（Kurrnavibhagah），又發現有《龜之特徵》（Kurmalakshanam），後者其後緊接著一章，其名稱與第二部《附篇》相同，即《山羊之特徵》（Chhagalakshanam）。

雖然從這些《旁經》（Parisishtas）中學習到的真正重要之事甚少，但它們的存在本身在印度心靈的進步與衰退的歷史中卻具有重要意義。如同在第一個或《韻律》（Chhandas）時期，我們看到雅利安人定居於印度，自由表達他們的思想與感受，從而無意識地創造了整個宗教、道德與政治觀念的世界；如同我們在第二個或《咒語》（Mantra）時期再次發現他們在精心蒐集他們的成果；在第三個或《梵書》（Brahmana）時期忙於系統化與詮釋已然變得費解且神聖的祖先之聲；如同在第四個或《經文》（Sutra）時期，我們看到他們全力投注於簡化梵書繁複的神學與儀式體系；那麼我們必將在這些《旁經》中認識到印度心靈的一個新階段，其特點突出鮮明，必須允許歷史性的解釋。《旁經》的目的在於提供那些在《經文》中被略過的神學或儀式觀點上的資訊，這很可能是因為它們未被視為具有充分的重要性，或者因為人們認為那些更直接相關的人士已經熟知這些內容。但最足以將《旁經》與《經文》區別開來的，正是這一點：它們以通俗且膚淺的方式對待一切事物；彷彿那個時代已然過去，當時的學生會花費十年或二十年的人生光陰於此。

在探尋婆羅門經（Brahmana）文獻的奧祕與掌握其繁複之處上。訴諸於《副經》（Parisishtas）等著作的一方，無異於進行一場必敗之戰。我們在其中已看不到充斥婆羅門經的那種自滿精神。婆羅門經的作者們相信，他們所言之事必當被信奉，他們所訂之律必當被遵守。他們對任何荒謬之事都毫不懼怕，「不可能」一詞似乎已從他們的字典中被逐出。在《經文》（Sutras）中，我們看到已發生了變化。其作者似乎意識到，他們所面對的大眾將不再容忍無盡的神學自負。婆羅門經中或許蘊藏著深刻智慧，其作者們或許確實相信他們所述之一切；但他們明顯是指望著弟子或讀者的順從態度，而這種態度只存在於被祭司或教授階級統治的國家中。《經文》的作者們已領悟到，人們不會聆聽智慧，除非它被包裹在明晰論證的外衣中，並用易懂的語言傳達。他們的著作涵蘊了婆羅門經中一切本質內容，但以實踐性、簡明與確定的形式呈現。這些著作寫成於婆羅門們與佛陀的流行學說進行初期戰爭的時代。他們尚未感到恐懼。他們的語言堅定有力，儘管不復浮誇。據伯諾夫（Burnouf）所言，「佛教很快發展成一套容易信奉的教義體系，在那些被婆羅門科學的困難所嚇倒的人們中獲得眾多信徒。在同一時期

佛教吸引了婆羅門中的無知者，它以開放的懷抱接納了各階級中的貧苦者與不幸的人。「為了消除或至少簡化其教義的困難之處，像商卡（Saunaka）與迦底耶衍那（Katyayana）這樣的人物採納了經文（Sutras）的新風格。一個民族的聖典文獻中的這類變化並非無謂而為，經文與婆羅門書（Brahmanas）相異之處在於，其目的無他，乃是為那些被過於繁複的論著所勸阻、且透過佛陀異端說教而發現了通往救贖之捷徑的人們提供實踐手冊。經文之後，除了附篇（Parisishtas）外，已無純粹吠陀特性的文獻。它們仍然預設了經文的法則與婆羅門書的信仰。尚無任何跡象表明濕婆（Siva）、毗濕奴（Vishnu）或梵天（Brahman）獲得了確定的至高地位。然而新的神祇被提及；俗民或流行的儀式被涉及其中。種姓變得更加鮮明並增加了。整個智識氛圍仍舊是吠陀式的，吠陀儀式、吠陀神學、吠陀語言似乎仍舊占據著附篇作者們的思想。任何曾為經文作者所忽略的細微事項，如今都被記載為具有重大重要性的問題。曾經認為一般性指導即為充足的主題，如今則在特別論著中被論述，目的在於服務那些不願再費力閱讀婆羅門祭祀全體系統的人們。經文那種技術性與嚴肅的語言被交換為自由流暢的風格，無論是散文抑或韻文；無論婆羅門人如何將附篇的作者

蘇特羅（Sutra）及若干帕里西斯塔（Parisishta）的著作，可以確定的是，任何已經掌握蘇特羅風格的人，絕不會屈尊採用帕里西斯塔那種草率的措辭。婆羅門的地位與特性所發生的變化，正如我們在蘇特羅中所見者，以及我們在帕里西斯塔中再次所見者，乃是迅速而決定性的。能夠撰寫此等著作的人們，深知自身的弱點，且可能已經歷過許多挫敗。他們周遭的世界正在朝向新的方向運動，古老的吠陀時代在無力的廢話中消亡而去。
